items.reality: PCA compresses variance, not necessarily label-relevant signal. Discarding components can remove predictive directions if they carry little variance but high class information.
items.example: A rare but predictive binary flag with low variance may load weakly on early components and get dropped when k is small.

items.myth: The first two principal components show the true clusters.
items.reality: PC1–PC2 maximize variance, not separation. Classes can align with later components or lie on non-linear manifolds invisible to linear PCA.
items.example: Two concentric rings have variance along radius (PC1) but class labels follow angle—a non-linear method may separate them better.

items.myth: PCA and SVD are unrelated techniques.
items.reality: For centered data, PCA directions come from SVD of X̃; libraries often implement PCA via SVD rather than explicit eigendecomposition of C.
items.example: `sklearn.decomposition.PCA` uses LAPACK SVD under the hood; manual `np.linalg.svd(X_centered)` recovers the same subspace up to sign.

items.myth: More components always mean better reconstruction.
items.reality: Adding components eventually fits noise; truncation trades bias (under-fitting structure) for variance in denoising and generalization.
items.example: Keeping all p components reconstructs training data exactly but offers no dimensionality reduction; k=3 may denoise while k=p overfits sensor noise.
